response:
1/6 America's very essence is intertwined with the spirit of the frontier – a place for entrepreneurs & rugged individuals eager to reinvent themselves. We've always been a nation that embraces the bold. #FrontierSpirit #AmericanIdentity

2/6 Think about the Gold Rush. It wasn't just bankers and boy scouts who opened the West. It was a whirlwind of speculation, driven by entrepreneurs, hucksters, and flawed characters seeking freedom from overreaching government. #GoldRush #RiskTakers

3/6 That "frenzied speculation" of the Gold Rush? It led to California becoming a state, built roads, churches, schools, and even the transcontinental railroad, binding us together. Progress often comes from unlikely places. #Innovation #Development

4/6 So when I hear calls to "protect" people from losing money speculating in #Bitcoin, I have to ask: Did we shield the 49ers from their losses? The vast majority lost everything, but that was the nature of the frontier. #Crypto #Speculation

5/6 It seems some have forgotten that this nation was built by those willing to take huge risks, not by those demanding protection from them. We didn't coddle the pioneers; we celebrated their audacity. #AmericanHistory #Entrepreneurship

6/6 America should lead, yes, but in a way that's true to our adventurous soul. We've never shied away from exploring a new frontier. Why should #Bitcoin or any other innovation be different? Let's not be afraid to explore. #LeadDontFear #Future

Thrilled to announce @usv is leading the Series E for Dave Morgan’s @Simulmedia! Our history goes way back – an investment in Dave's TACODA actually helped form USV. Doubly pleased to support him again as Simulmedia takes off. 1/4

For 10 years, Simulmedia has worked to bring internet advertising's efficiency to TV. It's only in the last year we've seen advertisers & media owners truly embrace this potential. Why the shift? 2/4

Two key drivers: 1) Advertisers are hitting a point of diminishing returns on some large online platforms. 2) TV targeting tech & premium video availability have advanced significantly. The tide is turning. 3/4

This market opening & Simulmedia's accelerating sales made it the perfect time for us to increase our commitment via our 2019 Opportunity Fund. Excited for what's next for TV advertising! #AdTech #VentureCapital 4/4

1/6: A first for us at Union Square Ventures: We're endorsing a presidential candidate - Hillary Clinton. Here's why this election compelled us to speak out.
2/6: As tech investors, we see innovation's power. But its benefits haven't reached everyone. Many feel left behind by automation & globalization, and we get the frustration.
3/6: We believe "more of the same" isn't the answer. Smart policy changes are crucial to ensure tech & innovation benefit all of society, not just a few.
4/6: Isolationism isn't a solution. We can't build walls against climate change, AI's impact, or global challenges. Cooperation and open dialogue based on science are key.
5/6: Of the major candidates, we believe only Hillary Clinton has the temperament and experience to lead effectively at home and represent us globally.
6/6: This election is too important. A protest vote is a wasted vote. We need to choose. For us at USV, the clear choice is Hillary Clinton. Let's all get out and vote!

1/7: Thrilled to announce our portfolio company @VeniamTech has closed a significant new funding round led by @VerizonVentures! This is a huge step towards realizing the promise of truly #smartcities. 🚀

2/7: What's @VeniamTech up to? They're connecting thousands of vehicles—both public and private fleets—to create dynamic, city-scale mesh networks. Think better, cheaper internet for commuters! 🚌💨

3/7: But it's more than just faster Wi-Fi. These networks will allow cities to affordably gather sensor data, enhancing security and optimizing transportation infrastructure. #IoT #UrbanMobility

4/7: @VeniamTech isn't just about cool tech; they deliver complete, deployable solutions today, backed by robust hardware, software, networking, and real-world operational experience.

5/7: This new funding will fuel @VeniamTech's growth in the U.S., Europe, and Asia, supporting deployments in global hubs like New York, Singapore, Barcelona, and London. 🌍

6/7: I'm genuinely excited to see these networks go live and the immediate benefits they'll bring. But honestly, what excites me even MORE is what we *can't* yet imagine...

7/7: The most thrilling prospect? The creative, entirely UNANTICIPATED uses of this pervasive connectivity that are bound to emerge over the next few years. That's where the real innovation will spark! ✨ #FutureTech #Connectivity

1/6 The internet feels meaner lately, right? Many see constant negativity. But is this its final form, or just an "ugly adolescence"? I believe we can build a better web, one that brings out the best in us.
2/6 That's why we at Union Square Ventures (@usv) are thrilled to invest in Koko! It's an innovative app using crowdsourced cognitive therapy to help manage stress & foster a more positive online experience.
3/6 Koko, from Rob Morris's PhD work at @medialab, proved in a clinical trial that everyday people can effectively help others reframe stressful situations – no professional training needed. It’s about collective well-being.
4/6 The most fascinating insight? Those *providing* support on Koko benefited the most! Helping others think more flexibly & positively about their lives reinforces our own ability to do the same. A true win-win.
5/6 Early Koko users feel it: "Every time I use the app...my ability to rethink situations gets stronger." It's about building a caring community & seeing how changing your viewpoint *changes everything*.
6/6 If you're dealing with stress (who isn't?), I encourage you to download Koko (iPhone now, Android soon!). Let's be reminded that the internet CAN make a real, positive, lasting difference. #MentalHealth #TechForGood

1/5 The Internet of Things is big, but what about the Internet of *Moving* Things? How do we keep drones, car sensors, and other mobile devices connected seamlessly? This has been a less-talked-about challenge.

2/5 Thrilled to share that our newest portfolio company, Veniam, is tackling this head-on! Their goal? To become the networking fabric for the Internet of Moving Things.

3/5 Imagine cities where vehicles themselves create a massive, reliable network. In Porto, Portugal, Veniam has already turned the entire public bus fleet into WiFi hotspots using their mesh technology!

4/5 The results? A staggering 72% of Porto's bus riders with smartphones now connect to the internet during their commutes. This isn't just theory; it's a proven reality, offering connectivity where cellular often struggles.

5/5 We believe Veniam's tech is more than just fleet efficiency. It's a glimpse into a future where mesh networks offer a resilient, cost-effective alternative to cellular for connecting everyone and everything on the move. Excited for this journey!

1/5: We at USV just made a big move: investing in Tucows, a public company! Why? Because the open internet, the bedrock of innovation, is seriously threatened. #OpenInternet #USV
2/5: Big carriers pushing their own services outside data caps? That's the sound of "permissionless innovation" fading. Startups can't compete when giants pay to play.
3/5: Tucows isn't just a great business; they're warriors for the open net. Now they're building community fiber networks, challenging the status quo. #FiberOptics
4/5: This is in stark contrast to incumbents who exploit monopolies, underinvest in local networks, hike prices & chase global deals, ignoring the communities they serve.
5/5: We're backing Tucows because they're building an internet where innovation isn't permission-based & access isn't throttled. Proud to join their mission! #InvestInOpen

1/9 So excited to share that Union Square Ventures has invested in @protocollabs! We're thrilled to back Juan Benet & team as they build crucial infrastructure for a decentralized web. This is about more than just tech; it's about a new wave of permissionless innovation. 🌐

2/9 The internet's early promise of openness has been challenged by consolidation & data control at the application layer. To reignite that spark, we need open protocols *further up the stack* – an open public data layer is key!

3/9 This is @protocollabs' audacious mission: creating these vital protocols. Think IPFS, which redefines how content is addressed on the web (directly, not by location!), and there's much more to come.

4/9 How do you fund open protocols that thrive on broad adoption, when traditional equity often favors closed systems? Crypto tokens are emerging as the native business model for open source, aligning incentives for the entire ecosystem.

5/9 Enter Filecoin: @protocollabs' token to support a decentralized storage network on IPFS. By funding with a token, those who create & consume value in the network (users storing data, providers offering storage) will directly benefit from its success.

6/9 And Filecoin aims to secure its network with *useful work* – actually storing files! This is a powerful shift from models like Bitcoin's, which consume vast energy. We can secure networks while respecting our planet's resources. 🌍

7/9 What's truly pioneering is @protocollabs' commitment to legal design. They're not taking shortcuts, but are meticulously working to create a Filecoin token pre-sale process that's defensible under U.S. law. This is a game-changer. 📜

8/9 This isn't just about Filecoin; it's about setting a responsible template for others. We believe this thoughtful approach to navigating complex regulatory landscapes is crucial for the long-term health and legitimacy of the entire token ecosystem.

9/9 We're backing @protocollabs because they're building powerful, broadly embraced protocols, ensuring value is shared widely, and laying a robust legal foundation for the future. Thrilled to be on this journey to re-decentralize the web! #OpenSource #Decentralization #Web3

1/n Thrilled to announce our investment in OB1, the company supporting OpenBazaar! We're betting on a future where marketplaces are truly decentralized, breaking away from models that concentrate power and control. #decentralization #OpenBazaar
2/n Many ask how OB1 will succeed without building the usual network-effect moats. The answer? They'll earn it. By offering valuable services to OpenBazaar users, competing openly, not by locking anyone in. It's about creating real value.
3/n We've seen platforms initially challenge incumbents, only to become new power brokers. OpenBazaar's protocol design aims to prevent this, ensuring no central entity can exploit the network for undue rent.
4/n This is our commitment to fostering innovation that empowers individuals. Start-ups like OB1 are crucial for challenging the status quo and unlocking new possibilities for creators and consumers everywhere.

1/n Investing in decentralized systems like OpenBazaar means confronting tough questions. A big one: discovery. How do users find what they need in a decentralized market without new gatekeepers emerging, as search did for the web?
2/n Honestly, we don't have all the answers for discovery yet. We're calling on the community to help us imagine and build models that maintain decentralization and prevent new concentrations of power. This is a shared challenge.
3/n And yes, we must address the potential for misuse. Open protocols, like the internet itself (TCP/IP, HTTP), can be used in ways we don't condone. But the overwhelming benefit of permissionless innovation is why we champion them.
4/n OB1 will not support illegal activities, but we can't design out all misuse without undermining the core freedom of an open protocol. We believe legitimate uses will dominate, and commit to advocating for responsible development.
5/n It's a journey. We aim to be transparent, foster responsible use of the OpenBazaar protocol, and lead by example. We're excited to work with OB1 and the community to navigate these complexities. #futureofcommerce #ethicsintech

1/6 Just finished Barbara van Schewick's "Internet Architecture and Innovation" – a crucial read for anyone concerned about the internet's future. It's not light reading, but the insights are vital.
2/6 The net neutrality debate is complex, with valid points on many sides. But Barbara's book offers a clear framework for understanding the core issues at stake for innovation.
3/6 Her core argument? The internet's architecture is changing, and ISPs' interests don't always align with users or app developers. Without intervention, innovation is at risk due to a lack of local loop competition.
4/6 Here's the truly groundbreaking idea: We need to think about policy at an *architectural level*. Instead of just managing behaviors, we should protect the internet's original design principles.
5/6 This isn't about overregulation. Regulating at the architectural level could be the *most efficient* way to ensure flourishing, competitive markets that *don't* need constant, heavy-handed oversight. A new paradigm!
6/6 It's a challenge to policymakers used to different approaches, but van Schewick makes a compelling case. If you're in this debate, you NEED to read this book. It could change how we protect online innovation.

1/4: We at USV don't usually invest pre-launch, but exceptions are made for extraordinary teams & visions. So excited to announce Tracked.com is now public – an investment we made a while back, backing veteran Mike Yavonditte. #VentureCapital #Fintech

2/4: Tracked.com is flipping the script on financial info. Yes, it has the data you expect, but also unique sources for insights into private AND public companies that others aren't showing you. #Data #Innovation

3/4: The truly novel part? It uses ad optimization tech *not* for ads, but to personalize *your* content. The more you use it, the smarter & more relevant the financial info becomes. A truly adaptive experience. #Personalization #AI

4/4: And the most innovative leap: a social media layer built into a financial service! Share insights publicly or privately, connect, and soon, embed charts. It's finance, made collaborative. Still early, but already a game-changer. Try it! #SocialFinance #Tracked

1/N: Been thinking about Apple, Facebook, & Twitter's recent moves. Calling them "companies" or "ecosystems" doesn't quite cut it. What if they're more like... governments? #WebGovernments
2/N: Why "governments"? They don't just *make* things; they create conditions for *others* to build & invest. Their users are citizens, developers are businesses in their economy.
3/N: An "ecosystem" doesn't have a central point of control that can change rules overnight. But Apple did with analytics. That's a government move.
4/N: So, Apple's OS 4 ToS changes? Less "clumsy control" & more a monarch protecting "state secrets" (like the iPad!) after an impulsive reaction. Privacy is the stated reason, but the monarch's will is clear.
5/N: Facebook, with its Credits & new privacy policy, looks a bit like Putin's Russia. Liberalized its economy (API), then reasserted state control. They say it's for citizen benefit, but that 30% tax via Credits is steep!
6/N: Twitter, by contrast, seems to have a more limited view of state power. Launching its own apps/URL shortener is like providing public infrastructure, not (yet) banning or taxing its "private sector."
7/N: One thing's for sure: these platform "governments" are NOT democracies. They're oligarchies. This makes data ownership ("property rights") & data portability ("emigration policies") absolutely critical for us users.
8/N: Craigslist offers a different model: "a government governs best that governs least." Fabulously profitable by being restrained. But even they face pressure from real-world governments.
9/N: Thinking this way raises new questions: What's Facebook's monetary policy (Credits)? Apple's foreign policy (proprietary vs. open standards)? Twitter's industrial policy? Their choices reveal their true nature.
10/N: Ultimately, understanding web platforms as governments helps us see how they balance state power vs. private initiative, and how well they serve their "citizens" & "economies." A useful lens for our times!

I'm all for promoting innovation, but I have to strongly disagree with Nathan Myhrvold's take on patents. When it comes to software and the web, the patent system isn't the solution; it's often the PROBLEM. 1/10

Nathan's idea of a "capital market for invention" might sound good on paper, but it doesn't align with how groundbreaking software and web services actually come to life. 2/10

The truth is, the independent software industry didn't boom because of strict patent enforcement. It thrived on PERMISSIONLESS INNOVATION, enabled by open operating systems and APIs that allowed developers to build without asking for approval. 3/10

Today, the software patent landscape is a nightmare. I've spoken to entrepreneurs who, despite exhaustive searches, couldn't identify potential patent infringements. How can you innovate if you don't even know who to ask permission from? 4/10

I've been investing in software and web services since 1993, and worked in startups since 1985. The narrative that software companies routinely ignore IP rights to cut costs or gain market share? I've *never* encountered that. It's a misrepresentation. 5/10

What I *have* seen, repeatedly, are innovative companies investing huge effort to build something new, only to be accused of infringement by patent holders they'd never heard of, often after achieving success. This isn't justice; it's a barrier. 6/10

It's telling that when proponents of the current patent system cite great inventions, they talk about light bulbs or new medical devices, not transformative software or web platforms. Even they seem to sense software patents are a different beast. 7/10

Software isn't like a physical object or a chemical compound. It doesn't cost millions to develop in the same way, nor does it require lengthy regulatory approval. Software patents are often abstract, trying to monopolize broad concepts. 8/10

When these abstract software patents are enforced, it's frequently against young companies that independently developed their technology, completely unaware of the patent. That's fundamentally unfair and stifles true progress. 9/10

Replacing a world of decentralized innovation on open platforms with one dominated by "intellectual property market makers" as new gatekeepers is not the way forward for software. We must preserve the ability to innovate without permission. 10/10

1/8 Patent trolls are waging war on innovation. Nearly a third of our portfolio companies are under attack, not for stealing, but for creating! These are brilliant teams blindsided by entities they never knew existed. #PatentReform #InnovationUnderSiege

2/8 The patent system is broken, especially for software. Abstract patents, sometimes on concepts as basic as the word "the" in software terms, are being weaponized. Naive examiners are approving overly broad claims.

3/8 Trolls hit startups hardest. Why? We can't afford a protracted legal fight & are forced to settle. This "pragmatic" extortion then funds bigger lawsuits against larger companies. The whole ecosystem suffers.

4/8 The ultimate irony? One troll made us provide *them* with the tech they accused us of copying – because they couldn't build it themselves! We're literally forced to dig our own graves. This isn't justice; it's a farce.

5/8 This isn't just about startups. Trolls even attack companies during M&A, disrupting growth and chilling investment in innovation. If this continues, we all lose a massive engine for progress.

6/8 My engineers *never* knew these patents existed. They solved problems independently. If multiple people arrive at a similar idea without copying, doesn't that prove it was obvious & shouldn't be patented in the first place?

7/8 Here's a simple fix: an **independent invention defense**. Shift the burden! Make trolls prove the accused *knew* about their tech. This one change could stop this predatory behavior cold.

8/8 Innovation is critical for social progress. We MUST fix the patent system. Supporting an independent invention defense is a crucial first step. Let's protect our creators. #SupportPatentReform #IndependentInvention

1/9 Tech startups can't afford to ignore politics anymore. As we've moved from infrastructure to applications, policy directly impacts our ability to innovate and reach the mainstream. We need to engage!

2/9 NYC is poised to be a global tech/media hub, driven by startups. But success has shifted from pure engineering to 'social engineering' – where NYC shines! Public policy can either fuel or kill this momentum.

3/9 Met with policymakers & NYC tech folks. Key concerns emerged: First, Net Neutrality. Startups *need* direct, unthrottled access to consumers. Worried that ISPs will discriminate, especially post-mergers like Comcast/NBCU. This stifles innovation.

4/9 Second, Immigration. Many of us are immigrants or rely on global talent. We need reform: how about a green card for any foreign STEM Masters/PhD grad? Let's attract the best minds!

5/9 Third, Consumer Protection. Yes, we need to protect consumers online, especially privacy. But hasty, uncoordinated government action can crush innovation. We need a predictable yet flexible framework.

6/9 And HUGE on everyone's mind: Patent Reform! Software & business method patents have become a *barrier* not a boon, especially for app-focused startups like many in NYC. It's strangling us.

7/9 Many of us fight broad software patents from entities we've never heard of. Some even have to license *their own tech* back to patent holders just to survive. This is absurd & hurts innovation!

8/9 These issues define a "web startup innovation" agenda – distinct from big science/big company innovation. Web startups are capital-efficient, emergent, and decentralized, like here in NYC.

9/9 We need YOUR help: 1) Define this agenda – what 4-5 things can gov do to help (or hurt) us? 2) Let's get organized and advocate for policies that let web startups thrive. Our voice matters!

1/5 In the venture world, we often say 'back the jockey, not the horse.' This couldn't be truer in today's rapidly transforming markets. It’s all about the right leader with vision.

2/5 Thrilled to be backing Dave Morgan again! He's one of the best 'jockeys' out there – a true talent for spotting opportunities & building incredible teams (Real Media, TACODA, anyone?).

3/5 And Dave's at it again with his new venture, Simulmedia! He’s tackling a massive opportunity: revolutionizing how we approach television.

4/5 Simulmedia is bringing sophisticated data analytics & targeting, pioneered online, to linear TV. Think smarter ad spend & connecting audiences with the shows they'll love.

5/5 Big things are ahead for Simulmedia. The TV industry is ripe for this kind of innovation, and Dave is the person to lead it. Can't wait to see them hit the market! #Simulmedia #TV #AdTech

1/6 Deeply concerned about the proposed Google/Verizon internet policy. While intentions may be good, I fear it’s a compromise that threatens the next generation of startups & the open internet as we know it. #NetNeutrality #Innovation

2/6 The internet's magic, allowing companies like Facebook & Twitter to boom, was its lack of gatekeepers. This proposal doesn't restore that core principle; it merely attempts to manage its erosion.

3/6 A huge red flag: On wireless, where so much innovation is happening, the plan offers *no real protections* for apps & services beyond "transparency." This leaves mobile users & developers vulnerable to ISP discrimination.

4/6 For wireline, the framework is complex & tricky to enforce. ISPs can discriminate if it doesn't cause "meaningful harm." This vague standard is a loophole that favors established players, not emerging ones.

5/6 Imagine a startup facing ISP discrimination. Can they afford a lengthy, costly fight? Unlikely. This proposal effectively shields incumbents & makes it harder for new companies to get funding & scale.

6/6 We need an internet that fosters innovation, not one where ISPs can pick winners. This proposal, as it stands, is a step backward, favoring established giants over the next disruptive idea. The future of online innovation is at stake.

1/3 Excited to share a must-read from Pinch Media! They just presented some fascinating insights into the iPhone App Store here in NYC. 📊

2/3 Pinch Media's got analytics from hundreds of apps, totaling over 30 MILLION downloads. That's a massive dataset giving us a real glimpse into app trends. 📈

3/3 If you're an iPhone developer, advertiser, or investor, you NEED to see this. A truly interesting picture of the app store ecosystem is emerging. Don't miss out! #iPhoneDev #AppStore

1/9
I've been saying it for a while: Open Spectrum is GOOD policy! We're drastically underusing our airwaves – like having a highway with only 5% of lanes open. We can do so much better. #OpenSpectrum #TechPolicy

2/9
Think of it like this: current licensed spectrum is like old-school circuit-switched phone lines – tying up a whole connection for one call. Open spectrum is more like the internet – packet-switched, incredibly efficient, and resilient. #Innovation #Efficiency

3/9
When we grant spectrum monopolies to big carriers, they act like... well, monopolies! They manage this shared resource to maximize their profits, not necessarily to drive down prices or speed up innovation for us, the consumers. #Telecom #Monopoly

4/9
The real magic happens in unlicensed spectrum! We see FAR more innovation there – just look at the number of FCC-approved devices. Think Wi-Fi, Bluetooth... all born from openness. #UnlicensedSpectrum #TechInnovation

5/9
Remember the iPhone App Store? Apple bucked carrier control & an explosion of apps followed. That's the power of an open platform. We need that same dynamic for our airwaves. #OpenEcosystem #Wireless

6/9
Some say we need monopolies to fund network buildout & R&D. I disagree. Network costs are falling, and open, internet-like networks are possible. Plus, big R&D labs aren't always the most efficient innovators for consumers. #FutureOfNetwork

7/9
Enlightened spectrum policy isn't about government meddling. It's about setting the right framework, like ARPANET did for the internet. The potential return? An innovation explosion rivaling the web. #Internet #EconomicDevelopment

8/9
That's why I'm critical of throwing billions at old telecom tech via stimulus. It props up incumbents & stifles the disruptive innovation that truly open spectrum could unleash. #Broadband #Stimulus

9/9
Let's create a VIBRANT market by opening up spectrum. It's not about losing auction revenue; it's about gaining a massive, lasting boost to our economy and quality of life for everyone. #PolicyChange #FutureFirst

1/7 Had a great chat with @DaveMcClure recently. It hit me: the flow of innovation has completely flipped! It's no longer NASA -> military -> enterprise -> consumer. It's the other way around. 🔄

2/7 Why this reversal? Because the success of today's web services hinges less on electrical engineering and more on *social engineering*. It's about how people connect and engage. #Innovation #Tech

3/7 Think about it. Services like Craigslist, Facebook, or Twitter are essentially lightweight governance systems. They thrive because users contribute content and help manage the community. It’s about empowering users, not just building complex tech.

4/7 Even services like Wesabe or Last.fm depend on users sharing data. Value is co-created. This is a fundamental shift from the old model where enterprise users were *trained* to use complex systems.

5/7 Today, consumer apps can't rely on captive audiences. They have to *seduce* users – hiding complexity, revealing features layer by layer, making the experience enticing, not intimidating. It's an art! ✨

6/7 This is why the best social engineers are now building consumer-facing services. They understand the massive leverage in creating systems people *want* to use, systems that can disrupt entire industries.

7/7 So, the innovation reversal isn't just a trend; it's a fundamental change driven by the power of user engagement and smart social design. The consumer is now leading the charge! #FutureOfTech #SocialWeb

1/2 Even with a small market share initially, it was clear the iPhone wasn't just another phone. It was a new platform, fundamentally changing the mobile game and shifting power away from carriers.
2/2 The release of the iPhone SDK? A game-changer! It's ushering in an era of open mobile development, paving the way for innovative services and a new wave of companies built to leverage its unique capabilities. So excited for what's next!

1/2 Why did we invest in Pinch Media? Because we see a tipping point: capable smartphones + quality mobile browsers + open dev environments (hello, iPhone SDK!) are about to make mobile services *better* than desktop.
2/2 Pinch Media is launching analytics for iPhone devs to help build this future. We're betting that unique features like location, P2P, and local storage will unlock incredible user experiences. Thrilled to be part of this journey!

1/6 After 15 years as a VC, I've noticed something intriguing: entrepreneurs who are radically open about their plans often seem to outperform the more secretive ones. It's just my sense, but there's a pattern. Why might this be? 🤔 #OpenStartup #Entrepreneurship

2/6 My hypothesis: Every time founders openly discuss their ideas, they usually learn MORE than they actually reveal. They're effectively concentrating insight, building a unique advantage, even if it seems counterintuitive to share your "secrets." 🤯 #Innovation #VentureCapital

3/6 Think of it: they bring deep context to each chat. Others offer fresh perspectives, but the entrepreneur can integrate these into their framework more effectively. It's like the rich in insight get richer. 💡 #StartupAdvice #KnowledgeSharing

4/6 This aggressive openness cultivates a powerful network – an "idea refinery." Data & information flow towards the entrepreneur, getting sharpened by knowledgeable people (nodes) along the way. The founder becomes the ultimate synthesizer. 🌐 #Networking #BusinessStrategy

5/6 Yes, being open with experts (who could be competitors!) is a double-edged sword: more to gain, more to lose. But if an entrepreneur is better prepared to execute, they still gain, even in an open exchange. Execution is a powerful differentiator. 🚀 #Startups #Execution

6/6 This "open" strategy is particularly potent in rapidly evolving markets, like the web, where adaptability trumps proprietary secrets. It's about being able to absorb, process, and capitalize on information better than anyone else. #TechTrends #Adaptability

1/9: People often ask about our investment strategy. Is it media? Early-stage? Consumer tech? The labels fit our current portfolio, but they don't explain the "why." Our real strategy? Arguing from first principles.
2/9: We believe the web is fundamentally transformative. Data is abundant, production can be voluntary, and distribution is nearly free. This is a seismic shift from the industrial economy.
3/9: Our core strategy is to arbitrage the gap between the web's immense capabilities and the readiness of existing economic/social structures to adapt. We're betting on this transformation.
4/9: So why early-stage, consumer-facing, web services in media *right now*? It's where the web's transformative power meets the least resistance and offers incredible capital efficiency.
5/9: Early-stage? Because the best web businesses are capital efficient & can gain pricing power quickly. But we're open to later-stage if it's truly market-transforming. It's about impact, not just stage.
6/9: Consumer-facing? Faster adoption. Enterprise often has gatekeepers resisting the market restructuring the web enables. We'd back enterprise if it bypasses gatekeepers & leverages web's "magic powers."
7/9: Web services, not just tech. We invest in user engagement & data insights, not tech for tech's sake. Sustainable tech differentiation is tough. UX and data smarts are key.
8/9: Media isn't our sole focus; it's just the first domino. Information markets are naturally disrupted first. Banking, education, healthcare, government – you're next. The principles apply broadly.
9/9: So while today we might look like "early-stage, consumer, web, media" investors, tomorrow we could be backing a later-stage, enterprise platform in healthcare. And it would be perfectly consistent with our first principles.

1/5 The initial excitement around Web 2.0's "collective intelligence" (think Flickr, Twitter) has matured. The core insight is well-trodden, making it tough for new services to break through the attention clutter. So, what's next for the web? #WebEvolution

2/5 Many agree the next step is real-world impact – using the web to tackle tangible problems, not just fuel an online attention economy. Collective intelligence must make us smarter & solve real issues.

3/5 But here’s a critical piece I believe is often overlooked: the web won't reach its full potential by just impacting the real world. It MUST also reach REAL PEOPLE – those who aren't necessarily early adopters or heavy web users.

4/5 This is about expanding beyond our tech circles. The web’s next significant leap will come from services that genuinely connect with and serve a broader, more diverse audience in their everyday lives.

5/5 At USV, we're betting on this. The future is about bridging cyberspace and real space, for everyone. That’s the innovation wave we’re excited to ride. #WebMeetsWorld #FutureOfTheWeb

1/5 Our portfolio company Adaptive Blue has been brilliant at using semantic analysis for 2 yrs to identify books, movies, etc., online, making web surfing faster. #SemanticWeb #Tech

2/5 Now, they've launched AB Meta! It's a dead simple, open format for any publisher or blogger to annotate their pages about 'things.' A fantastic bottom-up approach to complement their existing tech. #ABMeta #ContentDiscovery

3/5 Why is AB Meta a big deal? It's incredibly lightweight. Annotations go in the header, so ZERO changes are needed to your actual page content. So easy for creators! #Innovation #WebDevelopment

4/5 AB Meta isn't here to replace existing standards but to complement them. It works with RDF/Microformats (it's eRDF compliant!) but is far simpler for bloggers familiar with HTML, even if they don't code. #Accessibility #OpenFormat

5/5 The Web is for everyone now, and its potential & limits are clear. By making annotation accessible to more publishers with AB Meta, we believe Adaptive Blue will make the web more discoverable & valuable for all users. #FutureOfWeb #UserExperience

1/4 Some personal news from Wesabe: Jason Knight, our dedicated CEO, has made the difficult decision to step down. He's focusing on his family & newborn son's health, a decision we deeply respect.
2/4 Jason shared, 'Last month, my family welcomed the birth of our second child. He was diagnosed with a respiratory condition that requires ongoing medical attention... I need to focus my full attention on my family.'
3/4 We're incredibly sad to see Jason go after such exciting & rewarding years, but family comes first. We wish him all the best & hope for his son’s speedy recovery. Our full support is with them.
4/4 Stepping up as CEO is co-founder Marc Hedlund, our former CPO & product visionary. Jason has every confidence in Marc, as do I, to continue Wesabe's growth. Onwards!

1/5: We at Union Square Ventures are proud to endorse "Internet for Everyone"! This isn't about specific policies, but about embedding core principles into our digital future: Access, Choice, Openness, & Innovation. #InternetForAll
2/5: These principles: ACCESS for every home/biz to high-speed infrastructure. Real CHOICE of providers for lower prices/higher speeds. OPENNESS for free speech/commerce, no gatekeepers. And INNOVATION as an economic engine. #OpenInternet
3/5: Why is this initiative so crucial for us at USV? Because decentralized, open innovation isn't just good social policy – it's VITAL to our business. We invest in the internet's application layer. #VC #TechInvesting
4/5: Every company in our portfolio thrives on OPEN ACCESS – the freedom to put something on the Web without asking permission. This is the engine of web innovation, and we're here to champion it. #Innovation #Startups
5/5: The US created the Internet. An infrastructure built on Access, Choice, Openness, and Innovation will ensure we continue to realize its incredible potential for entrepreneurship and growth. #FutureOfInternet

1/7 The Census Bureau's $600M hand-held device failure for the 2010 census isn't just a tech glitch, it's a colossal screw-up. We're back to paper & pencil! 🤦‍♂️

2/7 This highlights a deeper issue: building purpose-built devices today is a broken process. We design from scratch, source anew, write custom software... it's slow, expensive, and risky.

3/7 Contrast that with web services! We use open-source stacks like LAMP, mash up services, & use scripting languages to build quickly & cheaply. Why can't hardware be like that?

4/7 That's the vision behind what we're doing at Bug Labs. We aim to create an architecture allowing anyone to quickly build a device & integrate it into a service, much like software.

5/7 Imagine rapidly mixing & matching components – camera, GPS, touchscreen – for any need. This modularity lowers the cost of failure, allowing cheap iteration as you define & refine.

6/7 Crucially, the hardware & software work you do is REUSABLE. If your first implementation is flawed, you can tweak, evolve, and not start from zero.

7/7 This makes far more sense than sinking another $600M into the same old, broken approach. It's time for a fundamental shift in how we build purpose-driven hardware.

1/7 Let's get real about "open" web services. By a strict definition – where anyone can take anything (code, data) & do anything with it, no permissions asked – they simply don't exist. Not even most open source truly fits this. #OpenWeb #Tech

2/7 Most systems, even those famed for openness like Craigslist or Google, have restrictions. The crucial thing isn't *if* there are restrictions, but understanding the *intention* behind them and their *effect* on innovation and users.

3/7 Some restrictions are bad, like trying to lock users in. This adversarial stance is unsustainable, especially when users build so much of a service's value. But some restrictions aim to protect the network from being wholesale copied.

4/7 The real challenge? Finding that sweet spot. Too few restrictions, and the community's integrity is at risk. Too many, and you stifle the very innovation at the edges that can make your service invaluable (think cool mashups!).

5/7 Here's a perhaps unconventional take: Maybe the ultimate goal isn't a *purely* "open" architecture. I live in Manhattan; it's managed. Stoplights restrict absolute freedom, but they enable us all to navigate the city. Structure can be enabling.

6/7 Language is another example. We have freedom in what we say, but less freedom to unilaterally change word meanings. This shared structure allows for communication and community.

7/7 So, let’s shift the debate from "is it open?" to "what's the right balance of freedom and structure?" We need to design systems that foster vibrant, innovative communities, prioritizing the community's interest. #Innovation #Community #WebServiceDesign

1/5 Just released the transcript for our Hacking Philanthropy event! It was a lively discussion with 40 people, no mics – so the transcript's a bit rough (Yochai Benkler's 'peer production' hilariously became 'bank lenders work on pure production' 😂). But the insights are too good to keep waiting!
2/5 Even with some garbles, I was reminded what a fantastic conversation it was. If you were there, it's a great memory jogger. If not, get ready for some serious insights! #HackingPhilanthropy
3/5 We saw the magic of community with our last transcript. Someone anonymously jumped in and beautifully reformatted it for everyone. That's the power of peer production in action! ✨
4/5 So, we're putting this one out there – warts and all. We believe it's better to share quickly & improve together. If you spot an error or have a correction (especially if you were there!), please jump in!
5/5 Whether it's fixing a typo like 'medidata' to 'metadata' or clarifying a key thought, your help makes it better for everyone. Let's make this transcript a truly collaborative resource! #OpenCollaboration #Community

1/9 Excited to share some reflections from our recent discussion on tech & philanthropy! A major theme? The rise of *markets* as an organizing principle in giving. This is a big shift from traditional models. #Philanthropy #TechForGood

2/9 Historically, philanthropy meant donors writing checks to orgs, who then did the work. Now, platforms like @DonorsChoose & @Kiva are acting like marketplaces, connecting donors directly to needs and projects.

3/9 Why now? Falling tech costs & ubiquitous connectivity make these marketplaces cheap to build & run. It's like Coase's theory of the firm in action: when transaction costs drop, markets can replace traditional orgs.

4/9 This isn't just about efficiency (though they can be *massively* more efficient – think Craigslist!). It's about fostering a more direct, immediate, and emotional connection to the impact you're making.

5/9 A key insight: these marketplaces empower users – teachers proposing innovative micro-solutions, students vividly describing project impacts. This "front-end production" often surpasses top-down program creativity.

6/9 This new model could be a true game-changer. The "Philanthropy 400" list of top philanthropies has been incredibly stable for decades. Marketplace nonprofits might just be the disruptive force to shake things up!

7/9 Of course, there are challenges. Ensuring a balance between donors & projects (market liquidity) is one. Another is tackling fraud, especially when many beneficiaries aren't online, limiting data for verification.

8/9 Unlike financial markets, defining & measuring impact in philanthropy is tough – there's no universal "currency" to compare diverse projects. This makes scaling and developing tools like securitization harder.

9/9 But I'm optimistic. The immediacy & empowerment of these platforms are powerful. As tech access (like mobile phones) grows globally & a new generation of donors emerges, the balance will shift further towards opportunity. #FutureOfPhilanthropy

1/7 For over a year, we've seen web-based personal finance as more than just "Quicken online." The real game-changer? The incredible asset hidden in our collective spending patterns. #PersonalFinance #Data

2/7 But this data is deeply personal. Any platform aggregating it *must* fiercely protect user privacy and acknowledge their ownership. This has been a core belief from day one. #Privacy #UserRights

3/7 Yesterday, our portfolio company @Wesabe unveiled something huge: the Wesabe Value Engine! It's a "Tips" tab supercharged with anonymous, aggregated spending data. #FinTech #Innovation

4/7 Imagine knowing how much your neighbors *actually* pay for a car service, not just reading a review. E.g., Weatherford BMW costs $700 more than Bavarian Professionals, with far lower user ratings! That's powerful. #ConsumerPower

5/7 Why is this different from review sites? Joshua Schachter (@joshu) nailed it: "How cool – you can’t spam it!" Real spending data is hard to fake, unlike subjective reviews. #TruthInData

6/7 Think about it: a 5-star review for a fancy restaurant is one thing. Knowing the reviewer ate there once, but hits the local Italian spot 5x a month? That tells a different, often more useful, story. #SpendingPatterns

7/7 Wesabe's Value Engine empowers YOU, the consumer, by making this transparent. It's about shifting the balance of power from merchants to informed individuals. And it only gets better with more data! #Empowerment

1/6 Beyond just insights, convenience matters. But not at the cost of your privacy. That's why @Wesabe's other big news, the Automatic Uploader, is so important. #DataSecurity #Trust

2/6 Users wanted automatic account updates, and understandably so! But for Wesabe, using a third-party aggregator was a non-starter due to our commitment to user privacy and control. #PrivacyFirst

3/6 So, Wesabe invested the time & money to build their *own* aggregation technology. This was crucial to uphold their Data Bill of Rights. #DataOwnership

4/6 What does this mean for users?
✅ No other provider stores a copy of your financial data.
✅ If you leave Wesabe, your data leaves with you.
✅ Full control, full transparency.
#YourDataYourRules

5/6 Building their own uploader also means Wesabe can offer an API. This ensures users who contribute their data aren't locked into Wesabe for innovative new services built upon it. #OpenData #FutureProof

6/6 By waiting to offer automatic uploads until they could do it *right* – without compromising user data ownership – Wesabe has truly reinforced its reputation as a trusted partner. This is how you build lasting relationships. #FinTech #TrustInTech

1/5: Just back from a panel that got me thinking: Who shapes the news you consume? It's not just about traditional editors anymore. #Web20 #Media

2/5: Heard the usual concerns: user-generated content lacks quality, no fact-checking. But then, a great point: post something wrong online, and the community corrects it FAST.

3/5: My big takeaway: The web *has* an editorial process. It's just flipped! It happens *after* publishing, out in the open for all to see. A messy, public, but transparent review.

4/5: Traditional media edits behind closed doors, often with hidden influences. The web's "edit" is public. You see the debate, the corrections. Ultimately, you decide what to trust.

5/5: It might seem inefficient, this "edit-after-publish" model. But like democracy, it's perhaps the worst form of editorial process, except for all the others tried. The power is with you. #TrustInMedia #DigitalAge

1/5 Today, we announced AOL/Time Warner is acquiring our portfolio co. TACODA. A bittersweet moment! So proud of Dave Morgan & the team for pioneering behavioral targeting – a true game-changer for online advertising. #AdTech #BehavioralTargeting
2/5 My journey with TACODA began with an elevator pitch from Dave Morgan in '95! He envisioned advertising to *people*, not just pages. That core insight was the bedrock of TACODA & the future of online ads. A seismic shift! #Innovation
3/5 TACODA truly embodied @usv's ideal: a tech-enabled service with market-changing potential, strong IT leverage, and a powerful data asset. They built the infra to deliver relevant ads at scale, while respecting user privacy. #VentureCapital
4/5 While we're sad to see this chapter close (TACODA had a great independent path!), the AOL TW + TACODA combo has the potential to create the dominant display ad network on the internet. They're geared up to finish what they started! #Acquisition
5/5 Huge congrats to the entire TACODA team! We'll miss working with you, but so excited to see you redefine advertising. Soon, targeting pages instead of people will be a distant, vague memory. Good luck! #StartupSuccess #Media

1/6 History doesn’t repeat itself, but it sure does rhyme! I've been thinking about the parallels between today's network operators and the mini-computer companies of the 80s. #TechAnalogies

2/6 Remember DEC, Prime, Data General? They competed on architecture, but missed the boat when standardized PCs, focused on price-performance, took over. They tried to save themselves with "value-added" solutions on old models.

3/6 Now, I see telcos, cable, & wireless co's making similar moves. Instead of laser-focusing on the best price-performance for connectivity (the "computer" of today!), they're pushing "value-added" services & proprietary architectural tweaks.

4/6 They seem to be resisting the decentralized innovation that built the PC and Internet. They're trying to extend the life of old rules, rather than competing under the new ones of openness and modularity.

5/6 My take? In 20 years, many big names in network operation today might be where the mini-computer giants ended up. The network IS the computer, and its architecture will be shaped by the same forces.

6/6 So, who will be the "Dell" of network service providers, focusing on low-cost, high-quality standardized services? My bet is on a new player, not an existing one. Time will tell! #FutureOfTech #Connectivity

1/7: I've learned that decentralized, user-centric innovation is a potent force. For years, I've wanted a very specific gadget for my bike rides, something the big companies would probably call a "freak" idea. But *I* want it.
2/7: Imagine a tiny, helmet-mounted video camera, always recording. Voice commands via a boom mic let me say "save last 60 seconds" because incredible moments are unpredictable. Like a black box for my bike! 🚴‍♂️📹
3/7: It's not just about the camera. I want a heads-up display! Visual feedback for commands, weather maps centered on my GPS location, even virtual street signs using a compass so I never miss that turn.
4/7: And connectivity? When I pass through a Wi-Fi cloud, I want those saved clips to automatically upload to my Tumblelog. Sharing the unique sights of my city rides, seamlessly.
5/7: Safety is key too. An accelerometer could detect a sudden stop (a crash!) and automatically SMS my wife my location and the last 30 seconds of video. Peace of mind on busy streets.
6/7: Some might say pieces exist, but it's the *integration* of these components and services for *my* specific, quirky needs that matters. I don't need to convince a big company it’s a mass-market hit.
7/7: Bug Labs is about giving people the platform to build these highly personal applications. It makes my dream device plausible. So, what application do YOU want to build? #UserInnovation #DIYtech

1/ Cash flow forecasting for VCs? It’s a completely different beast than it used to be, especially for those of us focused on early-stage web services. The old models just don't apply anymore. #VC #Startups (1/5)
2/ In the past, IT startups had a fairly standard path: develop, find distribution, then profit. Capital needs were predictable. Today's web services? They're lean, capital-efficient, and often built on network effects, not just defensible tech. (2/5)
3/ This shift makes predicting their capital requirements incredibly tricky. Even across a portfolio of 15-20 companies, forecasting liquidity is a much harder game now. (3/5)
4/ Think of a case like Del.icio.us. They proved immense value with very little capital. This allowed them to sell early, an outcome we couldn't easily predict using old frameworks. (4/5)
5/ So, what does this mean for us? At Union Square Ventures, we’ve adapted with smaller funds for more flexibility. But one thing's for sure: the playbook for cash flow forecasting has been fundamentally rewritten. (5/5)

1/ The capital efficiency of modern web services isn't just changing startups; it's forcing us VCs to rethink everything, especially cash flow forecasting and how we manage reserves. (1/5)
2/ Entrepreneurs can now test and prove their services with less upfront investment. This means they might opt for an early sale, or seek liquidity deals that let them stay independent, rather than take big dilution later. (2/5)
3/ This impacts our strategy. For example, at Union Square Ventures, we raised a smaller fund to be more nimble and flexible with initial investment sizes, adapting to this new landscape. (3/5)
4/ And the old M&A synergy playbook? It’s not as straightforward. A web service with a strong network effect might not see the same boost from being plugged into a large portal as traditional tech companies did. (4/5)
5/ All this makes me wonder: will these market shifts eventually change how VCs structure agreements with our LPs? The answer isn't clear yet, but forecasting cash flows is undeniably a new challenge. #VentureCapital (5/5)

1/5: Thinking about personal finance? Moving it to the web isn't just a tech shift, it's a *mindset* shift. It stops being "personal" and becomes "communal." Big difference! #PersonalFinance #WebEvolution
2/5: Desktop finance software is a lonely chore. But on the web? Your (anonymized!) data joins a community, unlocking collective wisdom. Suddenly, your finances are part of something bigger.
3/5: Imagine:
💡 See if your cable bill is higher than your neighbors'.
💰 Know which bank has better fees BEFORE you get dinged.
⭐ Pick services based on real community satisfaction ratings.
That's web-powered finance!
4/5: My favorite bit? Peer-powered data fixing! One person IDs a cryptic vendor on their statement ("ASDF Corp Inc." = "My Local Coffee Shop"), and *everyone* benefits. Smarter categories, less manual work! #DataCoop
5/5: This is the magic of the web: data sharing & re-use. It transforms personal finance into a collaborative tool to achieve your goals. This is why we're excited about services like Wesabe! #FinTech

1/5: When we scouted for a personal finance investment, we weren't looking for "Quicken online." We wanted a team that *truly* understood the web's power to transform. That led us to Wesabe. #Investment #FinTech
2/5: Wesabe got it: the value isn't just your data, but the *aggregate* community data. It's about real actions, what people *do*. As @joshu (del.icio.us) wisely said, "you can’t spam it." No faking financial habits!
3/5: Privacy & data ownership were HUGE for us. Wesabe's approach? Keep your bank logins ON YOUR DESKTOP, not their servers. Plus, full data export from day one. Your data, your control. Refreshing! #Privacy #DataOwnership
4/5: We've seen it with del.icio.us: authentic category creators win. Wesabe was already live, with users building that critical mass. In a network-effect world, that early lead is gold. They were *the* pioneers. #NetworkEffects
5/5: Marc Hedlund & Jason Knight weren't just building software; they were fostering a community to help people achieve financial goals. That vision, combined with their skills, made Wesabe the clear choice. Thrilled to be part of their journey! #Entrepreneurs

1/5 Thrilled to welcome Andrew Parker to the Union Square Ventures team! The story of how we found him is a bit unconventional, and I think, worth sharing.

2/5 We needed a new analyst. After initial outreach felt like we were missing something, a thought struck us: why not a blog post for an analyst search? Seemed crazy, brilliant, or maybe both! We'd never seen it done for this role.

3/5 The real breakthrough was Fred's idea: ask candidates to reply by commenting on the post, pointing us to their web presence – a blog, a site, a profile. This was key to managing responses and seeing genuine engagement.

4/5 We were looking for 'net natives,' and their online presence (blogs, etc.) was so much more revealing than a resume. When candidates came in, we felt we already knew them, allowing for deeper conversations from the get-go.

5/5 The quality of people who responded via comments blew us away. It made choosing tough, but ultimately led us to Andrew. A testament to finding talent by engaging with the community online!

1/8 The web's genius is its flatness – anything can link to anything. But this is also a huge limitation. Your computer doesn't get context like you do when you see a word like "Taurus." Is it a car, a bull, an astrological sign? #WebLimitations

2/8 We've all felt this frustration. Adding "car" to our search, or re-typing info into different sites because they can't understand what we were just doing. We need a web that understands *us* better. #SemanticWeb

3/8 Tim Berners-Lee envisioned the #SemanticWeb to solve this, adding a layer of meaning. Efforts like Microformats are important but painstakingly slow. AI to derive meaning from text? A tough nut to crack efficiently for every page.

4/8 Some create rich experiences within their own sites, like Amazon. Fantastic, but can lead to walled gardens and concerns about missing out on the broader web. Why can't that utility be everywhere?

5/8 This is where Alex Iskold and AdaptiveBlue come in. A year ago, Alex decided we couldn't wait. He took a pragmatic approach: lightweight semantic analysis, focused on popular objects, to anticipate what you might want to do next.

6/8 I was genuinely surprised by how often AdaptiveBlue teases out useful semantic info and offers relevant services. Reading a wine review? Instantly see similar wines or those from the same winemaker. That's powerful.

7/8 This is why we've made a Series A investment in AdaptiveBlue. We believe their services will simplify browsing, create meaningful recommendations, and enhance productivity. No need for site owners to add metadata – it works top-down!

8/8 What's truly exciting is the prospect of AdaptiveBlue developing tools for users to build the semantic web from the bottom-up, filling gaps. Thrilled to be in business with Alex Iskold and part of this journey! #Investment #FutureOfWeb

1/11 Ever noticed how the tech industry's battleground keeps shifting? It's not just about faster chips or cheaper storage. The very basis of competition evolves. #TechEvolution #FutureOfWeb

2/11 We've seen it move from hardware dominance to systems software, then applications, then networks, and more recently, to service quality (think uptime, security). Each step built on the last.

3/11 Today, especially in consumer web, it’s all about DATA. Services like Facebook & YouTube exploded not just due to slick features, but how they gather & use data. Data is the differentiator.

4/11 Craigslist is a prime example. We don't obsess over its hardware or uptime. Its value is the data – the listings – and the service *delivered by* that data.

5/11 So, with data seemingly at the top of the stack, the big question is: What’s next? Have we reached the end of this evolutionary journey in IT? I don't think so.

6/11 I believe the next major shift in competitive differentiation will be **Governance Systems**. It's not just *having* the data, but how you manage its acquisition and use.

7/11 Think about Craigslist's genius: its incredibly lightweight governance allows a tiny team to manage a massive global platform. That’s the power I’m talking about.

8/11 The governance system that provides the most utility for the most users with the least overhead will ultimately steward the largest communities and the most valuable data. #Governance

9/11 And looking even further ahead, beyond governance? If pressed, I’d guess the next layer will be **Values**.

10/11 Imagine two equally effective governance systems competing. One might champion openness & diversity, another might optimize for efficiency for a specific user group. Values become the differentiator.

11/11 After values? Perhaps aesthetics, or maybe then we do hit the "end of IT history." It's a fascinating progression to ponder. What are your thoughts on what's next? #FutureTech #BigIdeas

1/3 We don't just invest in improving existing markets; we invest in IT-enabled services with the potential to *change the structure of markets* themselves. Incremental improvements offer capped upsides.
2/3 Think Google vs. traditional TV ad sales. Google didn't just play the game better; they fundamentally restructured their part of the advertising industry, engaging 600k+ customers vs. a few hundred. That's transformative.
3/3 The real leverage for an enterprise lies in participating in or exploiting fundamental market change. We're looking for businesses that can rewrite the rules, not just play by them.

1/3 The mid-90s enterprise software boom taught us a valuable lesson. The best implementations didn't just link systems; they spurred major enterprise re-engineering to capitalize on integrated data.
2/3 Today, we're seeing the same phenomenon, but at the market level. Giants like Yahoo, eBay, Amazon, and Google have all, in their own ways, restructured their respective markets.
3/3 And it's far from over! The web services market is still in its infancy. With increasing information liquidity online, market cycles are shortening, paving the way for the next wave of equally disruptive services.

1/6 When it comes to IT-enabled services, not all investment is created equal. We're on the hunt for businesses with *substantial* technology leverage. It’s about smarts, not just spend! #ITLeverage #Startups

2/6 Think about it: Webvan was a massive grocery distribution center with a website. Huge capital in manual operations, very little real IT leverage. An expensive lesson in what *not* to do. #TechInvestment #BusinessModels

3/6 Now, contrast that with Craigslist. Just 21 employees serving 300 markets, 10M unique visitors, 9M posts monthly! That's the power of IT leverage in action. #Efficiency #Scalability

4/6 The secret sauce for Craigslist? They coordinate their *users'* activities. Users post ads, monitor abuse. The platform empowers the community to build and maintain itself. Genius! #UserGeneratedContent #CommunityPower

5/6 Del.icio.us curates links the same way – by coordinating user activity. Social navigation & user-generated content, powered by an efficient web service, are prime examples of IT leverage. Others? Crawling, collaborative filtering. #SocialNavigation #TechInnovation

6/6 So, we look for that substantial IT leverage. Often it's in services that cleverly coordinate user activities, but we also find it in innovative IT uses that might not even have a social component. #Innovation #SmartTech

Just had a mind-melding coffee with Craig Newmark (yes, *the* Craigslist Craig!). It hit me: in today's web service world, Customer Service IS the New Marketing. Sounds a bit out there? Stick with me... (1/8)

Why? Because your users aren't just "users." They're your co-contributors, your co-creators! Think about services like Craigslist, YouTube, Flickr – they're built *by* the community *for* the community. (2/8)

So, trying to "sell" to your co-creators doesn't make sense. Instead, *serve* them. This isn't just about support; it's about deeply listening and learning what they truly need to make the service better for them. (3/8)

This active service *is* your marketing. It's how you discover unmet needs & iterate. Forget long dev cycles & big launch pushes. Get a basic version out fast! Your users will guide its evolution. (4/8)

We saw this with del.icio.us – put a simple tool out there, and our community not only told us what to improve but, given the chance, BUILT incredible add-ons themselves! That's user genius unleashed. (5/8)

Key takeaways for building this way:
➡️ Launch early & iterate based on real use.
➡️ Don't try to "buy" the market. True adoption is organic, pulled by users.
➡️ Open up your data! Users see it as theirs, and it fuels more innovation. (6/8)

This transforms users from passive consumers into active partners. Their success *is* your success. This deep engagement is far more valuable and sustainable than any traditional marketing campaign. (7/8)

The million-dollar question: If web services are ad-supported, and marketers *also* adopt this "service as marketing" idea, cutting ad spend... what then? Food for thought for my next post! 😉 (8/8)

1/5: Just hosted "Union Square Sessions 2," diving deep into public policy's impact on innovation. We gathered academics, policy pros, VCs, & entrepreneurs for a frank discussion. #Policy #Innovation
2/5: Initial perspectives were telling. Entrepreneurs felt they could navigate any regulatory environment, while VCs, accountable for returns, leaned towards shifting investments over fixing "inappropriate" policy.
3/5: However, the dialogue sparked a shift. VCs & entrepreneurs left with a clearer view of the risks of poor regulation AND how enlightened policy has historically fueled innovation in the US, and continues to do so abroad.
4/5: The learning was mutual! Policy experts & academics also gained a better appreciation for the "irrepressible optimism" of entrepreneurs and the "mercenary logic" of the VC world. Bridges built!
5/5: No single consensus emerged, but everyone gained a richer understanding of what's at stake and the range of solutions. This cross-sector dialogue is vital for a thriving innovation ecosystem. Stay tuned for more insights!

1/5 The current Net Neutrality debate feels like stepping Through the Looking Glass! Up is down, down is up. Innovators (Amazon, Google) want regulation, while infrastructure giants (Verizon, Time Warner) champion the "free market." #NetNeutrality

2/5 This mind-bending switcheroo only clarifies when you consider wireless broadband. Suddenly, infrastructure co's want protection from competition, and app co's welcome new players. Their true colors emerge!

3/5 So, what's really at stake with #NetNeutrality? It's about whether the local loop is a natural monopoly, if it will always be, and if so, should it be regulated for the public good. That’s the core question.

4/5 At our Sessions event, we all agreed: letting telcos & cable co's exploit their monopolies to tax app providers or control user experience would stifle innovation and harm consumers. No easy agreement on the solution, though.

5/5 We're in a massive societal shift, like agrarian to industrial. Expect no easy answers for #NetNeutrality. Solutions will need fresh thinking, blending regulation, market mechanisms, & innovation. It’s complex, but vital.

1/5 Let's talk "innovation" in the #NetNeutrality debate. Pipe companies claim regulation stops them innovating their infrastructure. But is it true innovation, or just deploying tech invented years ago? #TechPolicy

2/5 Consider this: many other countries (Korea, Japan, Europe) already have faster broadband. Our pipe providers could invest in disruptive tech like wireless, but their huge sunk costs in wired networks make them hesitant. Understandable, but not progress.

3/5 Laurie Racine's point at our Sessions event was spot on: we're seeing "defensive innovation" – reacting to the duopoly – not "creative innovation" that flourishes without such constraints, like it does elsewhere. A crucial distinction.

4/5 The consensus at our event was clear: allowing current local loop monopolies to dictate terms would hurt true innovation at the applications layer and ultimately, consumers. But we couldn't agree on the exact fix.

5/5 The path forward for #NetNeutrality isn't old dogma. It'll be a mix: some traditional regulation, some market forces, and a heavy dose of technical & business model innovation. We must step away from easy orthodoxies for these new challenges.

1/10 The landscape of defensibility in tech has radically shifted. What once protected companies is no longer the primary fortress. Let's talk about the new moat.
2/10 Back in the day, for IT infrastructure pioneers, patents & IP were everything. Protecting massive R&D investments was key when building the tech we rely on now.
3/10 But for modern IT-enabled *services* businesses, the old playbook doesn't quite fit. Relying solely on patents for defensibility? Not the main strategy anymore.
4/10 So, what's the new cornerstone of defensibility? It's all about DATA. Proprietary data assets are becoming the most valuable moats in the digital services world.
5/10 Consider giants like eBay or Craigslist. Their strength isn't just their brand, but their vast listings – a core data asset. Plus, their reputation & spam systems improve *with more data*, creating a virtuous cycle.
6/10 At @usv, we've seen this firsthand. Companies like TACODA (a past portfolio co.) built their edge by understanding anonymized user behavior *across* an entire network, not just on isolated sites.
7/10 This network-wide data view creates immense value. But it's not just about serving relevant ads. The real kicker? Uncovering *unexpected correlations* in user behavior and adapting to their changes over time. That's a deep data advantage.
8/10 And it's not just about the data itself. The operational know-how, the 'hard-won experience' of managing huge datasets in real-time and making tough trade-offs (cost vs. revenue from data) – that’s a massive competitive barrier.
9/10 Today's best internet services are built *with* their users. They evolve through a constant dialogue, shaped by user interactions. The data generated is continuously fed back, making the service better for everyone.
10/10 This dynamic, data-driven feedback loop is where true differentiation and defensibility lie. I'm constantly amazed by the creative ways founders are leveraging user data to build these powerful, evolving services. It's the future.

1/9 A year ago, we shifted our investment thesis to be an evolving dialogue. Today, I want to share a constant in our approach: our focus on Information Technology Enabled Services.

2/9 We stick to IT because it's what we know best. For startups, deep market understanding trumps generic investment knowledge. This conviction guides us.

3/9 We don't typically invest in companies selling tech. We back those *using* tech internally to deliver services – think Google, not just a component vendor.

4/9 The game of selling tech directly is tough. Mature markets, huge R&D costs, and then big players often squeeze you or demand OEM deals. Hard to win there.

5/9 An investor friend in comms equipment told me: "Only 5 buyers, they want massive innovation, then tell Cisco to OEM it. Nothing left for the startup." Sound familiar?

6/9 IT enabled services, however, are capital efficient. They can reach customers directly online, skipping costly distribution channels. That's a huge plus.

7/9 The IT enabled services market is still young. Business models aren't set in stone, and financing isn't 'codified' like in core tech. This means less competition for early deals.

8/9 So, why IT enabled services? 1) We know the space. 2) It's an emerging market full of disruption. 3) Fewer investors chasing the same deals (for now!).

9/9 Some question the scale & defensibility of IT enabled services. Valid concerns! We're thinking hard about how to find scalable & defensible businesses here. More on that soon.
